content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcar PRACrrcEs CouirarssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
August 15,2007
David Bauer
2150 River PlazaDr., Suite 150
Sacramento, California 95 83 3
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-122
Mr. Bauer:
Re:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the new provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act')l related to state officeholdeiaccounts-and
committees. You have requested advice on behalf of Board of Eq;rllization member Bill
Leonard.
QUESTTONS
Mr. Leonard is an elected member of the Board of Equalization. Mr. Leonard has
not filed and does not plan to run for any future elective office at this time. Can
Mr. Leonard use contributions to his offrceholder account to pay for the following:
1. Travel (including that of Mr. Leonard's spouse) to speak about California tax
policy before partisan organizations such as local Republican groups.
2. Travel (including that of Mr. Leonard's spouse) to California Republican party
semi-annual conventions (Mr. Leonard is a delegate to those conventions by virtue of his
office).
3. Coffee service for attendees to California Republican Party conventions during
which attendees may discuss the function of the Board of Equalization.
4. Storage of records associated with campaigns for prior elections.
5. Cellular or other telephone service and Intemet service.
I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, Sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. A-07-122
Page No. 2
6. A website featuring commentary on california public and tax policy.
7. costs of producing, framing, and sending "Friend of Taxpayer" awards
presented in special recognition of service by taxpayers.
8. Costs of computer supplies and service associated with producing and sending
a weekly electronic newsletter dealing with public policy issues.
ANALYSIS AND CONCLUSION
Proposition 34 introduced into the Act a prohibition against post-election
fundraising by candidate's for elective state office unless the candidate uses the funds to
pay net debt. In 2006, Senate Bill 145 (Stats. 2006, Ch. 624,urgency) created another
nilrow exception to the post-election fundraising ban to permit a person holding elective
state office to raise contributions after an election to use for officeholder purposes.
Section 853160) expressly restricts the use of these contributions to the payment of
fficeholder expens es. Specifi cally, Section 853 1 6(b) provides:
"[A]n elected state officer may accept contributions after the date
of the electronfor the purpose of paying expenses associated with holding
the ffice provided that the contributions are not expended for any
contribution to any state or local committee. Contributions received
pursuant to this subdivision shall be deposited into a bank account
established solely for the purposes specified in this subdivision.,,
[Emphasis added.]
The Commission has also adopted Regulation l853l.62,which elaborates on the
permissible uses of officeholder contributions raised under Section 85316(b).
The Act also contains provisions governing the general use of campaign funds,
including campaign funds raised for officeholder purposes. (Sections 895i0- ZSSZZ.I
These sections are known as the "personal use" provisions. The..personal use,,
provisions of the Act were designed to prevent candidates, elected officials, and others
who control the expenditure of campaign funds from benefiting personally from their
campaign activities. These rules also apply to the use of officeholder contributions.
Thus, in analyzing your questions, we apply a two-tiered approach.
o First, we determine if the expenditure of officeholder funds is "associated with
holding the office" held by the state elected officer as governed by Section
85316(b) and Regulation 18531.62.


File No. A-07-122
Page No. 3
o If the expenditures are permissible under these provisions, we next determine if the
expendifures are permissible under the Act's "personal use" provisions in Sections
89510 -89522.
A. Expenses Associated with Holding Office
An elected officer's duties can include a wide arcay of activities. Therefore, a
determination of whether a particular activity is associated with holding that office
requires a case-by-case analysis. In conducting this analysis, we believe that so long as
the predominant purpose of the activity is reasonably related to the duties of the office,
the activity is "associated with holding the office." On that basis, we consider each of the
activities posed in your question.
7. Travel (including that of Mr. Leonard's spouse) to speak about California
tasc policy before partisan organizations such as local Republican groups.
As a member of the Board of Equalization, it would appear the activity is related
to the duties of the office that Mr. Leonard holds. A public official can be engaging in
the duties of his or her office even when the activity is before a partisan political
organization.
2. Travel (including that of Mr. Leonard's spouse) to Caldornia Republican
ParA semi-annual conventions (Mn Leonard is a delegate to those conventions by
virtue of his office).
3. Coffee service for attendees to Caffirnia Republican Parer conventions
during which attendees may discass thefunction of the Board of Equalization.
Despite being a delegate to the Califomia Republican Party by virtue of his
position on the Board of Equalization, there appears to be no reasonable relationship
between this expenditure and the duties of the office Mr. Leonard holds. As mentioned
above, there may be a reason in some situations for Mr. Leonard to attend even a partisan
political organizatton's meeting for fulfilling his duties as a public official.
However, attending a meeting to act as a delegate conducting that organization's
business or providing others with food or beverages when attending for that pu{pose
would not appear to be associated with Mr. Leonard's position as a member of the Board
of Equalization. In addition, the fact that attendees at the meeting may discuss functions
of the Board of Equalization would not alter the fact that the predominant purpose of this
attendance is to conduct the partisan organization's business and not that of the Board of
Equalization.
Therefore, these expenditures would not be permissible out of officeholder
contributions under Section 853 1 6.


File No. A-07-122
Page No. 4
4. Storage of records associated with campaignsfor prior elections.
This expenditure does not appear related to holding office as a member of the
Board of Equalization. Therefore, it would not be appropriate for Mr. Leonard to expend
officeholder contributions raised under Section 85316 for this putpose.
5. Cellular or other telephone service and fnturnet service.
Expenditures of officeholder contributions raised under Section S5316(b) for
telephone and internet services would be permissible if Mr. Leonard's predominant use
of these services related to his duties as a mernber of the Board of Equalization.
6. A website featuring commentary on California public and tax policy.
It would appear this expenditure is related to Mr. Leonard's duties as a member of
the Board of Equalization.
7. Costs of producingrframing, and sending oFriend of Taxpayer" awards
presented in special recognition ofservice by taxpayers.
While this activity may generally relate to tax matters, this does not appear on its
face to relate to Mr. Leonard's official duties as a member of the Board of Equalization.
However, our conclusion would differ if this were an official Board program.
8. Costs of computer supplies and service associated with producing and
sending a weekly electronic newsletter dealing with public policy issues.
See the conclusion concerning question 5 above.
Finally, please note that Regulation 18531.62(d)(1) prohibits the use of
officeholder contributions raised under Section 85316(b) for "campaign purposes" as
defined in Regulation 18525(a). under Regulation 18525(a), "campaign purposes"
include:
"(1) Payments for fundraising and campaign strategy expenses for
election to a future term of office.
"(2)Payments for mass mailings, political advertising, opinion
polls or surveys, and other communications in connection with election to
a future term of office. For purposes of this section, a mass mailing,
political advertisement, opinion poll or survey, or other communication
shall be considered "in connection with election to a future term of office"
if it makes reference to the officer's future election or status as a candidate


FileNo. A-07-122
Page No. 5
for a future term of office, or if it is made by an incumbent officer within 3
months prior to an election for which he or she has filed any of the
following:
"(A) A statement of intention to be a candidate for a specific
office, pursuant to Government Code Section 85200.
"(B) A declaration of candidacy or nomination papers, as specified
in Chapter I (commencing with Section 8000) of Division 8 of the
Elections Code).
"(C) Any other documents necessary to be listed on the ballot as a
candidate for any state or local office.
"(3) Payrnents for services and actual expenses ofpolitical
consultants, the campaign treasurer and other campaign staff, pollsters and
other persons providing services directly in connection with a future
election.
"(4) Payments for voter registration and get-out-the-vote drives.,,
Thus, to the extent that any expenditure for the activities about which you inquire
are expenditures for matters listed in Regulation 18525(a), then officeholder
contributions cannot be spent on these activities.
B. Personal Use Law
Assuming it is permissible to expend officeholder contributions raised under
Section 85316(a) for these activities, we next determine if the Act's "personal use" rules
permit the same.
The general rule under the Act's "personal use" rules is that an expenditure of
campaign funds must be reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose. However, where an expenditure of campaign funds confers a substantial
personal benefit on the candidate or officeholder, the expenditure must be directly related
to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.5.) Since under
Section 85316(b) as discussed above, officeholder contributions may only be spent on
activities associated with holding office, the "personal use" rules apply blcausi in
Mr. Leonard's case, he will spend the funds for a governmental purpose.


File No. A-07-122
Page No. 6
1. Travel (including that of Mr. Leonard's spouse) to speak about Caffirnia
tax policy before partisan organizations such as local Republican groups.
The personal use law sets forth specific standards conceming certain types of
expenditures. Section 89513(a)(1), which deals with travel, provides:
"(a) Campaign funds shall not be used to pay or reimburse the
candidate, the elected officer, or any individual or individuals with
authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a
committee, or employees or staff of the committee or the elected officer's
govemmental agency for travel expenses and necessary accommodations
except when these expenditures are directly related to a political,
legislative, or goveflrmental purpose.
"(1) For the purposes of this section, payments or reimbursements
for travel and necessary accoflrmodations shall be considered as directly
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose if the payments
would meet standards similar to the standards of the Internal Revenue
Service pursuant to Sections 162 and274 of the Internal Revenue Code
for deductions of travel expenses under the federal income tax law.
"(2)For thepurposes of this section, payments orreimbursement
for travel by the householdt2l of acandidate or elected officer when
traveling to the same destination in order to accompany the candidate or
elected officer shall be considered for the same purpose as the candidate's
or elected officer's travel."
Thus, as applied to Mr. Leonard's use of officeholder contributions raised under Section
85316(b), the test set forth in Section 89513(a)(1) is whether there is a direct relationship
between a govefirmental purpose and the travel in question. Internal Revenue Code
Section 162(a)(2) sets forth general rules that permit the deduction of all ordinary and
necessary traveling expenses while away from home in the pursuit of a trade or business.
Pursuant to this section, Mr. Leonard may use campaign funds for "business portions" of
the trip. To the extent that any day is not a business day (such as days on which there are
no meetings or formal activities), campaign funds may not be used. Moreover, an
officeholder may not use officeholder contributions for expenditures that are solely
personal in nafure, such as certain meals, entertainment, or sightseeing activities.
Since the travel expenses you describe appear to be governmental in nafure,
campaign funds may be used for ordinary and necessary expenses associated with the
travel and accommodations consistent with the lnternal Revenue Service standards for
the deduction of "business expenses" in connection with your position.
2 For these purposes, o'household" is defined as "the candidate's or offrceholder's spouse,
dependent children, and parents who reside with the candidate or elected officer." (Section 8951 l(bx4).)


File No. A-07-122
Page No. 7
2. Cellular or other telephone service and fnturnet service.
Since the specific provisions in the personal use law do not provide any guidance
regarding such expenditures, the general rule enunciated in Section 89512 applies.
AccordinglS an officeholder may use campaign funds for these expenditures to the
extent they are reasonably related to a governmental purpose.
3. A website featuring commentary on caldornia public and tax policy.
This expenditure appears to relate to a governmental purpose and thus would be a
permissible use of officeholder funds.
4. Costs of computer supplies and service associated with producing and
sending a weekly electronic newsletter dealing with public policy issues.
Since the specific provisions in the personal use law do not provide any guidance
regarding such expenditures, the general rule enunciated in Section 89512 applies.
Accordingly, Mr. Leonard may use officeholder funds to purchase such supplies, since
such expendifures are reasonably related to be a govemmental purpose.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
W. Wallace
General Counsel
Division
Jwwjgl
